questions.stem: Both rosette patterns shown here—one a radial arrangement of cells around a central fibrillar core, the other a ring of cells surrounding a true central lumen—are classically encountered in which pediatric tumor?

explanation.summary: Homer Wright rosettes (radial cells around a fibrillar center) and Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes (cells around a true lumen) are both characteristic histologic features of retinoblastoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: Homer Wright rosettes (radial cells around a fibrillar center) and Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes (cells around a true lumen) are both characteristic histologic features of retinoblastoma.

questions.stem: This graph plots the prevalence of RAS gene family mutations across many human cancer types. In which malignancy are RAS-family (KRAS) mutations found at among the highest frequencies?

explanation.summary: KRAS mutations are extremely common in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, one of the highest-prevalence tumor types shown; the graph illustrates the variability of HRAS, KRAS, and NRAS mutation frequencies across cancers.
explanation.whyCorrect: KRAS mutations are extremely common in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, one of the highest-prevalence tumor types shown; the graph illustrates the variability of HRAS, KRAS, and NRAS mutation frequencies across cancers.

questions.stem: This histologic field shows malignant cells forming irregular duct-like glandular spaces in the pancreas. This KRAS-driven, smoking-associated, nearly always fatal neoplasm is best identified as which entity?

explanation.summary: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma forms infiltrative duct-like glandular structures composed of neoplastic cells, commonly harbors KRAS mutations, and carries a very poor prognosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma forms infiltrative duct-like glandular structures composed of neoplastic cells, commonly harbors KRAS mutations, and carries a very poor prognosis.

questions.stem: The left panel shows a brown, irregular skin lesion and the right panel a neoplasm containing brown pigment. This pigment is a clue to the transformed cell of origin. What is this neoplasm, commonly driven by activating BRAF mutations?

explanation.summary: Cutaneous melanoma arises from transformed melanocytes, which produce brown melanin pigment visible histologically; activating BRAF (V600E) mutations are a common oncogenic driver and therapeutic target.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cutaneous melanoma arises from transformed melanocytes, which produce brown melanin pigment visible histologically; activating BRAF (V600E) mutations are a common oncogenic driver and therapeutic target.

questions.stem: This hypercellular bone marrow shows increased granulocytes with eosinophils and small, hypolobated megakaryocytes. These findings are characteristic of which myeloproliferative neoplasm driven by a BCR::ABL fusion?

explanation.summary: CML marrow is hypercellular with granulocytic hyperplasia, eosinophilia, and small hypolobated megakaryocytes; it is driven by the BCR::ABL fusion producing constitutive tyrosine kinase activity.
explanation.whyCorrect: CML marrow is hypercellular with granulocytic hyperplasia, eosinophilia, and small hypolobated megakaryocytes; it is driven by the BCR::ABL fusion producing constitutive tyrosine kinase activity.

questions.stem: These paired histologic fields compare a benign polypoid glandular proliferation (left) with a malignant lesion (right). What feature defines the lesion on the right as adenocarcinoma rather than adenoma?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deposition of osteoid by the tumor cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: A polypoid growth confined above the muscularis mucosae

choices.label: C
choices.text: Restored nuclear polarity and absent atypia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Invasion of neoplastic epithelium through the basement membrane into the stroma

choices.label: E
choices.text: Formation of true rosettes with central lumina

explanation.summary: Colorectal adenomas are non-invasive polypoid growths, whereas adenocarcinoma is defined by invasion of the neoplastic epithelium beyond the basement membrane into the lamina propria and deeper tissue.
explanation.whyCorrect: Colorectal adenomas are non-invasive polypoid growths, whereas adenocarcinoma is defined by invasion of the neoplastic epithelium beyond the basement membrane into the lamina propria and deeper tissue.

questions.stem: A patient exhibits the flat, evenly pigmented skin patches (left) and the soft cutaneous tumors of peripheral nerve (right) pictured here. These findings are diagnostic of which condition?

explanation.summary: Café-au-lait macules and multiple cutaneous neurofibromas are hallmark manifestations of neurofibromatosis type 1, caused by loss of the NF1 GTPase-activating protein on chromosome 17.
explanation.whyCorrect: Café-au-lait macules and multiple cutaneous neurofibromas are hallmark manifestations of neurofibromatosis type 1, caused by loss of the NF1 GTPase-activating protein on chromosome 17.

questions.stem: This wheel arranges eight acquired capabilities—such as evading growth suppressors, resisting cell death, and inducing angiogenesis—around a central tumor. What do these eight traits collectively represent?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The phases of the cell cycle

choices.label: E
choices.text: The components of the WNT pathway

explanation.summary: The figure depicts the eight fundamental hallmarks of cancer—the capabilities neoplasms acquire to sustain growth, evade suppression and immune destruction, and invade and metastasize.
explanation.whyCorrect: The figure depicts the eight fundamental hallmarks of cancer—the capabilities neoplasms acquire to sustain growth, evade suppression and immune destruction, and invade and metastasize.
